title: Colouring clay body with dry pigment
subtitle: Kneading mineral or acrylic pigments into air-dry or polymer clay
excerpt: A technique for adding colour throughout a clay body by kneading dry pigment or acrylic paint into air-dry clay before forming. The colour runs through the entire thickness of the piece, not just on the surface. Works with iron oxide powders, dry acrylic pigments, or artist's acrylic paint.
sourceNotes: Pigment addition to clay bodies is a longstanding practice across multiple ceramic and craft traditions. Technique derived from first principles.

glossaryTerms.definition: Kneading clay to homogenise moisture, expel air, and distribute additives evenly through the body.

content.text: Colouring a clay body before forming means the colour runs throughout the thickness of the finished piece, not only on the surface. Knead the pigment into the clay by
wedging
: this spreads the colour evenly, just as wedging spreads moisture. Use red or yellow iron oxide powder for earthy tones, or artist's acrylic paint for a wider range of colours.

attrs.content: Wear an N95 mask when working with dry pigment powders. Avoid inhaling any fine powder. Once the pigment is fully incorporated into wet clay, no further dust hazard applies.

content.text: Pigment types and ratios
Red iron oxide: 1-3% by weight of clay for a warm terracotta tone; 5-8% for a deep burnt-orange.
Yellow iron oxide: 2-5% for ochre-yellow tones.
Artist's acrylic paint: 1-2 tablespoons per 100 g of clay. Too much acrylic affects the clay's working properties.
Step by step: adding dry pigment
Wearing an N95 mask, weigh the clay and calculate the pigment amount. Flatten the clay into a thick disc.
Sprinkle the pigment powder across the top face of the clay disc.

Fold the clay over the pigment and begin kneading on the board. Work the pigment in using a press-fold-press-fold motion. As the clay opens, streaks of colour appear. Continue until the streaks disappear and the colour is even throughout.

Cut through the clay with a wire cutter and check the cross-section for streaks. If streaks remain, fold and knead for longer. Fully distributed pigment shows an even colour right through the cut face.
Step by step: adding acrylic paint
Squeeze the required amount of acrylic paint onto the board. Press the clay directly over the paint and begin kneading.
The paint and the clay's water content work against each other initially. Keep kneading; after two to three minutes the paint is fully incorporated.
Check the cross-section as before. Acrylic-coloured clay will be slightly stiffer than plain clay; if too stiff, add a few drops of water and knead further.
What it looks like when it is right

A cross-section through fully pigmented clay shows an even, streak-free colour throughout. The surface colour matches the interior. When the finished, dried piece is sanded, the cut edge shows the same colour as the face.
Common mistakes
Adding too much pigment. More than 8% by weight of iron oxide weakens the clay body.
Not kneading long enough. Streaky clay gives patchy colour in the finished piece.
Forgetting the mask when working with dry iron oxide powder. The colour itself is not hazardous, but inhaling any fine mineral powder repeatedly is not advisable.
